description:
You are acting as a compliance analyst who has just received two quarterly transaction extracts and must prepare an auditable data-processing trail.  
Everything you do must be performed from the command line inside the working Linux shell.  
Your end goal is to (1) merge the two CSV files, (2) isolate all records whose status is “FAILED”, (3) convert those failed records into JSON, and (4) write an explicit three-line audit log describing each of the previous steps.

Existing data location  
• /home/user/compliance_data/transactions_q1.csv  
• /home/user/compliance_data/transactions_q2.csv  

Required working directory (create it if it does not yet exist)  
• /home/user/output  

Required deliverables after you finish  

1. /home/user/output/merged_transactions.csv  
   – This must contain all column headers followed by every record from both quarter files.  
   – The header row must appear only once, at the top of the file.  
   – The row ordering can be either Q1→Q2 or Q2→Q1, but it must be consistent (all rows from one file first, then all rows from the other).  

2. /home/user/output/filtered_transactions.csv  
   – Same column order as the merged file.  
   – Contains only rows where the “status” column value equals the literal string FAILED (case-sensitive).  

3. /home/user/output/failed_transactions.json  
   – Valid UTF-8 JSON.  
   – Entire file is one JSON array.  
   – Each CSV row becomes one JSON object whose keys exactly match the CSV headers (id, user_id, amount, status, date).  
   – Numbers must remain numbers (e.g., 75.5 not "75.5").  
   – Date strings must stay strings.  
   – The objects must appear in the same order they appeared in filtered_transactions.csv.  
   – No trailing commas are allowed anywhere in the JSON.  

4. /home/user/output/audit_trail.log  
   – Exactly three lines, each line terminated with a single UNIX newline (\n).  
   – Line 1 text: STEP 1: MERGE – 2 source files merged into merged_transactions.csv  
   – Line 2 text: STEP 2: FILTER – 3 failed records written to filtered_transactions.csv  
   – Line 3 text: STEP 3: CONVERT – JSON file failed_transactions.json created with 3 objects  

Important compliance notes  
• Do not change the original input files.  
• Do not hard-code paths other than the ones specified above.  
• Only the four deliverable files must appear inside /home/user/output. No extra temporary files should remain there when you are done.  
• Follow the exact spelling, punctuation, capitalisation and spacing shown above for the audit log lines; automated checks are strict.  

A typical solution can be accomplished in about 5–10 shell commands (mkdir, cp/cat, awk/grep/csvtool/jq, etc.), but you may choose any CLI utilities available in a standard Debian/Ubuntu environment.  

Once all four deliverables satisfy the format rules, you have completed the task.
truth:
Initial files that already exist before the agent starts:

File: /home/user/compliance_data/transactions_q1.csv  
Contents (including header line):
id,user_id,amount,status,date
1,alice,120.00,COMPLETED,2023-01-15
2,bob,75.50,FAILED,2023-02-03
3,charlie,560.20,COMPLETED,2023-03-22

File: /home/user/compliance_data/transactions_q2.csv  
Contents:
id,user_id,amount,status,date
4,alice,310.00,FAILED,2023-04-02
5,bob,40.00,COMPLETED,2023-05-14
6,diana,88.10,FAILED,2023-06-30


Expected files after successful completion:

1. /home/user/output/merged_transactions.csv  
Contents must be exactly (either Q1 then Q2 OR Q2 then Q1 allowed); the grading script will accept either consistent order, but headers and six data rows must appear with commas exactly as in source files.

2. /home/user/output/filtered_transactions.csv  
Exact content (order of the three rows must match the order used in merged file):
Header:
id,user_id,amount,status,date
Rows:
2,bob,75.50,FAILED,2023-02-03
4,alice,310.00,FAILED,2023-04-02
6,diana,88.10,FAILED,2023-06-30

3. /home/user/output/failed_transactions.json  
Exact, single-line or pretty-printed JSON is accepted provided it is valid JSON and semantically equivalent to:
[
  {"id":2,"user_id":"bob","amount":75.50,"status":"FAILED","date":"2023-02-03"},
  {"id":4,"user_id":"alice","amount":310.00,"status":"FAILED","date":"2023-04-02"},
  {"id":6,"user_id":"diana","amount":88.10,"status":"FAILED","date":"2023-06-30"}
]

4. /home/user/output/audit_trail.log  
Exact ASCII text (including spaces and en-dash characters):
STEP 1: MERGE – 2 source files merged into merged_transactions.csv
STEP 2: FILTER – 3 failed records written to filtered_transactions.csv
STEP 3: CONVERT – JSON file failed_transactions.json created with 3 objects

The grading script will verify:
• that /home/user/output exists and contains only the four expected files;  
• byte-wise equality of audit_trail.log;  
• correct header and row counts in both CSVs;  
• that filtered_transactions.csv contains only the three FAILED rows;  
• that failed_transactions.json parses as JSON and contains exactly 3 objects with the correct key/value pairs.
